Madam Bhikaji Cama

[Azaadi](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/azaadi/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Madam Bhikaji Cama

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Sep 24, 2024

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!wYqS!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1f95604b-6613-4a7e-b82a-09b0de7f023f_403x668.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!wYqS!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1f95604b-6613-4a7e-b82a-09b0de7f023f_403x668.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Sttugart, Germany, 1907

The International Socialist Congress was underway at the industrial city in Germany, attended by 1000 representatives from across the world. Among the many delegates was an Indian lady in a colorful saree, whom many thought to be some kind of princess.

India was burning with the fire of revolution against the British rule, who in turn tried to stem the rising tide of nationalism with a series of draconian bills, ordinances and repression. And when her turn came, the fraily looking lady, boldly unfurled the Indian flag, defiantly proclaiming.

*This flag is of Indian Independence! Behold, it is born! It has been made sacred by the blood of young Indians who sacrificed their lives. I call upon you, gentlemen to rise and salute this flag of Indian Independence. In the name of this flag, I appeal to lovers of freedom all over the world to support this flag.*

That frail Indian lady was Madame Bhikaji Cama, revolutionary leader, close associate of Veer Savarkar and a truly remarkable lady. Born on September 24, 1861 into a well to do, Paris family in Mumbai. Her father Sorabji Patel, was one of the city's leading merchants, also an educationist, philantrophist too.

Though the recipient of English education, she had strong nationalist feelings from a young age, and was a born rebel. A polyglot who used her flair in different languages to advocate the cause of Indian independence.

She was married to Rustom Cama, one of the leading lawyers of Mumbai at the age of 24. However her husband's pro British leanings, clashed with her own nationalist ideology. That ideological chasm between Bhikaji and Rustom Cama wasn’t merely a domestic rift—it was emblematic of the divide cutting through Indian society. Many within the elite, particularly those who benefited from British patronage, clung to the illusion of benevolent imperialism. Bhikaji, however, saw through it.

Her decision to walk away from that life—of status, security, and social conformity—was a revolutionary act in itself. While the marriage grew cold, her convictions only burned brighter. Her illness and travel to Europe in 1902, ostensibly for treatment, became her escape hatch into a wider world of revolutionary politics. Once in London, and later Paris, she chose exile over compromise, and activism over comfort.

It was in London that Bhikaji Cama met Dadabhai Naoroji and inspired by his ideals plunged into the freedom movement. Dadabhai Naoroji’s moderating wisdom gave her ideological grounding, but it was her associations with the likes of Shyamji Krishna Varma and Lala Har Dayal that sharpened her into a revolutionary force.

Those booklets and pamphlets she produced—meticulously crafted, often printed in secrecy—were powerful weapons of dissent. They carried across oceans the stories of Indian suffering, the demand for self-rule, and the voices that British censorship tried to silence. Her declaration, *“India is for Indians!”* was more than rhetoric—it was reclamation.

And then to see her carry this mission to the United States, far from the comfort of her birthplace and the corridors of her past—what courage that took. She understood international opinion mattered, that colonial empires thrived in the shadows of ignorance, and so she illuminated the truth wherever she went.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Ws5d!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F397fd660-b9b2-42ad-b585-f3b02e131899_459x294.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Ws5d!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F397fd660-b9b2-42ad-b585-f3b02e131899_459x294.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

On August 22, 1907, when Bhikaji Cama unfurled that flag at the Stuttgart Conference, she wasn’t just hoisting cloth—she was raising the very idea of a free India before the world. The flag’s design, with eight lotuses for the provinces, flowers for the princely states, and *Vande Mataram* inscribed in bold Devanagari, was a tapestry of unity and defiance. The collaboration with Veer Savarkar and Shyamji Krishna Varma made it a collective act of resistance, stitched together by visionaries in exile.

Today, that very flag—smuggled into India by Indulal Yagnik—is preserved at the Maratha and Kesari Library in Pune, a quiet sentinel of a stormy past. It stands not just as a relic, but as a reminder of a woman who dared to speak for a silenced nation.

The British could not stop her, inspite of their best efforts, they asked her a to give an undertaking that she would be allowed to return to India only if she stopped taking part in nationalist activities, she refused point blank, and preferred to stay in exile,fighting for India's freedom from abroad.

Her refusal to sign that undertaking was more than a personal decision; it was a declaration that her conscience could not be colonized. She chose exile over silence, and in doing so, transformed Paris into a crucible of revolution.

The Paris Indian Society, founded in 1905 by Cama, S.R. Rana, and Munchershah Burjorji Godrej, became a sanctuary for Indian nationalists fleeing British surveillance. It wasn’t just a political hub—it was a training ground, a pressroom, and a haven. They published *Bande Mataram* and later *Madan’s Talwar*, smuggling these fiery texts into India through French territories like Pondichéry. Cama even financed the printing of *Talvar* in Berlin, ensuring the revolutionary flame never dimmed.

And yes, her home in Paris reportedly welcomed revolutionaries from across the globe—including Lenin himself. That intersection of Indian nationalism and global socialist is a testament to Cama’s vision transcending borders; she wasn’t just fighting for India, but for the oppressed everywhere.

Her support of Savarkar during his exile in Paris was pivotal. It was in her home that he penned *The First War of Indian Independence*, a radical reinterpretation of the 1857 uprising. When British publishers recoiled, it was Madam Cama who arranged for its printing in the Netherlands, disguising it in *Don Quixote* covers to smuggle it into India—a literary Trojan horse that carried fire in its belly.

And when the British banned *Vande Mataram*, she didn’t just protest—she *resurrected* it. Her Paris edition of the magazine, launched in 1909, became a beacon of resistance, continuing the spirit of Sri Aurobindo’s earlier Calcutta publication. Through its pages, she exposed colonial atrocities, celebrated martyrs, and called for unity and rebellion.

*Madan’s Talwar*, her other publication, was born from grief and fury after Madan Lal Dhingra’s execution. Published from Berlin and edited by Virendranath Chattopadhyaya, it aimed to stir unrest and awaken the sepoys of the British Indian Army. Both magazines were banned in Britain and India, yet she smuggled them through French territories like Pondichéry, often hiding them in innocuous packages.

That moment at Marseilles in 1910 was a crucible of heartbreak and heroism. Bhikaji Cama’s plan to help Savarkar escape British custody was daring in every sense—he leapt from the porthole of the *SS Morea* into the waters of the French port, hoping to claim asylum. But fate intervened. A delay in reaching the rendezvous point meant he was recaptured by French police and handed back to the British. For Cama, who had orchestrated the escape with such care, this failure was a wound she carried for the rest of her life.

The British, furious at her involvement, seized her property in England and pushed for her extradition. But France, valuing its sovereignty and perhaps moved by her cause, refused to hand her over. Still, the cost was steep—she was forced into deeper exile, her ties to home severed.

And then came Lenin’s invitation—a gesture of solidarity from one revolutionary to another. Yet, true to her principles, she declined. Her fight was for India, and she would not be swayed by ideological alliances that might dilute her mission.

However inspite of all the British harassment, Madame Cama never wavered in her stand at all. When WWI broke out, she refused to support em, and asked the Indian soldiers-" Would you fight for some one who chained your motherland?

But with France aligning with Britain during WWI, her sanctuary turned hostile. The Paris Indian Society, once a vibrant hub of revolutionary thought, was forcibly disbanded under pressure from both British and French authorities.

S.R. Rana and his family were deported to Martinique, a remote French colony in the Caribbean. And Madam Cama herself was placed under house arrest in Bordeaux, required to report to the police daily—a humiliating routine for a woman who had once addressed the world from Stuttgart.

Yet even in confinement, she remained unbroken. Her exile became a crucible, her silence a form of resistance. She refused Lenin’s invitation to the Soviet Union, choosing instead to endure isolation rather than dilute her mission.

Her feminism was deeply intertwined with her nationalism. She believed that women were the soul of the nation, shaping its future not just through activism but through the very act of nurturing and educating. Her words—*“the hands that rock cradles also build persons”*—are a poetic assertion of women’s agency in shaping society.

Yet, she was also pragmatic. For her, India’s political liberation was the gateway to all other freedoms. She envisioned a future where, once the chains of colonialism were broken, women would naturally rise to claim their rightful place in the nation’s destiny. Her stance wasn’t a dismissal of women’s rights—it was a strategic prioritization, rooted in the belief that true emancipation could only flourish in a free land.

After 33 years in exile, Bhikaji Cama’s return to India in November 1935 was not triumphant, but deeply symbolic. Stricken by a paralytic stroke, she was no longer the firebrand who had once defied empires—but her spirit remained unbowed. With the help of Sir Cowasji Jehangir, she was allowed to return, not as a revolutionary, but as a fading elder who had earned her final glimpse of the land she never stopped fighting for.

She passed away on August 13, 1936, at the Parsi General Hospital in Mumbai, her legacy largely unsung in her own time. And yet, in death as in life, she gave selflessly—bequeathing most of her personal wealth to the Avabai Petit Orphanage for Girls, now known as the Bai Avabai Framji Petit Girls’ High School. It was a final act of faith in the future she had long envisioned: one where Indian girls would grow up free, educated, and empowered.
